data.title: TikTok U.S. valued at $14B in proposed deal, below analyst estimates
data.description:
President Donald Trump has cleared a proposal to allow TikTok to continue operating in the U.S., in a deal that Vice President JD Vance said would value the app's U.S. operations at around $14B.

Under the proposed deal, TikTok’s U.S. business will be majority-owned by an American investor group. Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) will serve as TikTok's security provider.

The full composition of the investor group is yet to be finalized. Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), Silver Lake and Abu Dhabi-based MGX will reportedly be the main investors, while ByteDance (BDNCE) will hold less than 20% in TikTok U.S.

"Ultimately, the investors are going to make the determination about what they want to invest in and what they think is the proper value," Vance said, who also noted that there was "some resistance on the Chinese side."

The $14B valuation is well below some analyst estimates. In April, Wedbush analyst Dan Ives estimated that TikTok was worth around $100B with the algorithm, or $30B-$40B without it.

The proposed deal "could be the most undervalued tech acquisition of the decade," Ashwin Binwani, founder of Alpha Binwani Capital, told Bloomberg. He estimated that the $14B valuation reflects only a third of TikTok's true value.

"By every major financial metric and peer comparison, this price tag looks dramatically misaligned with reality," he added.

Trump's executive order provides a 120-day window to complete the deal. It's unclear if China will approve the proposal.

data.title: Concentrix drops 21% as focus remains on margin recovery, tariff impact
data.description:
Shares of Concentrix (NASDAQ:CNXC) dropped 21% in premarket trading as the technology and services company fell short of estimates for quarterly profit, even as revenue surpassed expectations, while investors focused on margin recovery and investment returns amid tariff headwinds. 

The company's guidance for full-year revenue and EPS narrowed and was slightly reduced versus the previous quarter, and margin improvement is now projected as a multi-quarter process rather than immediate.

Margin shortfalls in Q3 were attributed to excess capacity from tariff-related client impacts and accelerated transformation programs, whereas Q2 margin softness was mainly due to labor retention during client pauses.

"And what happened was, with some of the additional noise with tariffs within the third quarter, by the second month, we still weren't seeing that uptick coming through from the clients. The clients weren't seeing that uptick either," CEO Christopher Caldwell said.

"There was a bit of additional noise before August on tariffs, frankly."

The company's management, however, conveyed that revenue growth outpaced guidance based on AI-driven integrated offerings and continued market share gains.

Management remains confident in the long-term strategy and expects the iX suite of software to become accretive as the year ends, while capital returns to shareholders are maintained through an increased dividend and ongoing share repurchases. 

Ahead of the earnings, Agar Capital noted that Concentrix (NASDAQ:CNXC) remains undervalued, trading at just 4.4x forward earnings despite strong fundamentals, robust cash flow, and operational AI solutions.

data.title: Lexaria Bioscience stock soars after ending $5M stock sale agreement with JonesTrading
data.description:
• Lexaria Bioscience (NASDAQ:LEXX) rose 32% in premarket trading on Friday after the company announced the termination of its Capital on-Demand Sales Agreement with JonesTrading Institutional Services LLC, effective September 19.
  • As of the date of termination, the company had sold an aggregate of 14,995 shares under the agreement for gross proceeds of $38,236.00.
  • The deal, originally signed on August 21, 2024, and amended on February 5, 2025, had authorized Lexaria (NASDAQ:LEXX) to sell up to $5 million worth of its common stock through JonesTrading, acting as either sales agent or principal.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: INTC, GFS, and more
data.description:
Stock futures recorded slight gains in premarket trading Friday, with market attention squarely focused on U.S. PCE inflation data set for release later today, which could influence the Federal Reserve’s next policy steps.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) +4%, GlobalFoundries (NASDAQ:GFS) +4% - Chipmakers rallied after the WSJ reported the Trump administration is weighing a policy requiring U.S. firms to manufacture as many chips domestically as they import. Companies falling short of the 1:1 ratio could face tariffs, the report said.

Biggest stock losers

  • Concentrix (NASDAQ:CNXC) -21% – Shares plunged after Q3 results showed a profit miss despite largely in-line revenue. Adjusted EBITDA margin fell 180 bps Y/Y. Q4 revenue is projected at $2.525B–$2.55B (vs. $2.53B consensus), with non-GAAP EPS of $2.85–$2.96, missing the $3.32 estimate. Full-year revenue guidance of $9.798B–$9.823B was in line, but EPS of $11.11–$11.23 fell short of the $11.69 consensus.

data.title: Li Auto launches Li i6, a five-seat battery electric SUV
data.description:
• Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI) on Friday officially launched Li i6, a five-seat battery electric SUV.
  • Li i6 is priced at RMB 249,800 for its standard configuration. Deliveries of Li i6 will commence on September 27, 2025.

data.title: Trump’s 100% drug tariffs send pharma stocks lower
data.description:
European and Asian pharmaceutical stocks fell Friday after President Donald Trump unveiled sweeping new tariffs, including a 100% levy on branded and patented drugs starting October 1, with exemptions for companies actively building new manufacturing facilities in the U.S.

The plan could significantly increase costs for drugmakers lacking U.S. production facilities, intensifying pressure on those that have not yet begun construction. The new duties could hit about $220 billion of U.S. pharmaceutical imports and lift the average tariff rate by 3.3 percentage points, Bloomberg Intelligence economists estimate.

Markets had been bracing for the long-flagged pharmaceutical tariffs from Trump, but drugmakers with major U.S. exposure tumbled across Europe, Tokyo, Seoul, and Hong Kong.

In early trading, Swiss drugmakers Lonza (OTCPK:LZAGY), Novartis (NYSE:NVS), and Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF) slipped about 1.2%, Germany’s Merck (OTCPK:MKGAF) and Bayer (OTCPK:BAYRY) fell 1.1% and 1.5% , respectively; while in Japan, Otsuka Holdings (OTCPK:OTSKY) (OTCPK:OTSKF) dropped 2.9%, Daiichi Sankyo (OTCPK:DSNKY) (DSNKF) lost 2%, Chugai Pharmaceutical (OTCPK:CHGCY) fell 4.7%, Takeda Pharmaceutical (NYSE:TAK) edged down 0.1%, and Shionogi (OTCPK:SGIOY) gained 1%.

The EU pharma sector is the biggest supplier of pharmaceutical products to the U.S., with leading European drugmakers such as Novo Nordisk (NVO), GSK (NYSE:GSK), Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY), and AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN). U.N. Comtrade data shows that about 60% of U.S. pharmaceutical imports in 2024 came from the European Union, with Switzerland—outside the EU—ranking second at 9%.

To offset the impact of looming tariffs, several major drugmakers, including AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), Eli Lilly (LLY), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Novartis (NYSE:NVS), and Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY), have unveiled multi-billion-dollar investment plans in the U.S. this year.

Merck (MRK), Novo Nordisk (NVO), and Eli Lilly (LLY) have launched U.S. construction projects since 2023 in states including Delaware, North Carolina, and Texas to strengthen domestic supply chains and support top-selling cancer, diabetes, and immunology drugs. AbbVie (ABBV), which co-markets cancer drug Imbruvica and makes the immunology blockbuster Skyrizi, has plans to begin an expansion of its Illinois facilities this fall.

ETFs to watch: SPDR S&P Pharma ETF (NYSEARCA:XPH), VanEck Pharma ETF (PPH), Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV).

data.title: Federal agencies told to prepare for mass firings if government shuts down
data.description:
The White House has directed federal agencies to prepare for mass firings if the government shuts down on October 1, targeting programs whose funding will expire by the deadline and have no alternative funding source.

The Office of Management and Budget also instructed agencies to prepare reduction-in-force notices for employees in programs that are not consistent with President Donald Trump's priorities.

"RIF notices will be in addition to any furlough notices provided due to the lapse in appropriation," OMB wrote in a memo, first reported by Politico.

"Once FY 2026 appropriations are enacted, agencies should revise their RIFs as needed to retain the minimal number of employees necessary to carry out statutory functions," it added.

OMB noted that if Congress successfully passes a "clean" continuing resolution to fund the government before the deadline, the steps in the memo won't be necessary.

In the past, federal workers were furloughed during government shutdowns till a budget deal was reached, after which they'd return to work.

Programs that would continue regardless of a shutdown include Social Security, Medicare, law and immigration enforcement, veterans benefits, military operations and air traffic control, an OMB official told Politico.

"This is an attempt at intimidation," Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-NY) said about OMB's directive. "These unnecessary firings will either be overturned in court or the administration will end up hiring the workers back, just like they did as recently as this week."

Federal funding is set to end on September 30. The Republican-controlled House passed a stopgap measure to fund federal operations through November 21 without Democratic support.

The Senate needs at least seven Democratic votes to pass the measure. Democrats' demands for the funding bill include an extension of Affordable Care Act subsidies.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Clean Harbors prices $745M senior notes due 2033; increases secured loan facility
Stock index futures rise as key inflation gauge looms
data.description:
Stock index futures rose on Friday, amid a key inflation gauge slated to be released today, as discussions on rate cuts continue to be in focus.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.3%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.2% and Dow futures (INDU) +0.3%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.18%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 3.65%.

Wall Street finished lower in a roller coaster session on Thursday as the broader market averages observed selling pressure after jobless claims fell and GDP was revised higher in the third estimate.

“Markets continued to struggle yesterday, with a broad-based selloff that saw the S&P 500 post a third consecutive decline. The main catalyst was a strong batch of U.S. data, which meant investors dialed back their expectations for rapid Fed rate cuts and pushed front-end Treasury yields higher,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said. 

That U.S. data was the big market driver yesterday, as it painted a more resilient economic picture than previously thought, which undercut some calls for faster rate cuts, Allen said. 

The Fed's preferred inflation gauge—the PCE price index—will land before the bell. 

The core PCE price index excludes food and energy prices, which can swing from month to month. For August, the consensus of economists expects a 2.9% Y/Y increase in core PCE, unchanged from July. Meanwhile, the consensus projects a 0.2% M/M rise vs. +0.3% in the prior month.

Including food and energy prices, the PCE price index is expected to increase 2.7% Y/Y, ticking up from the 2.6% Y/Y rise in July. Month over month, it's expected to grow 0.3%, up from 0.2% in the previous month.

The September consumer sentiment will be released during market hours.

Forecasters see no revision from the preliminary readings, with the sentiment index unchanged at 55.4 and inflation expectations unrevised at 4.8%.

On tariff news, President Donald Trump announced sweeping new tariffs, including 100% on branded and patented pharmaceutical drugs, to take effect on October 1, with an exemption if the company is actively building a new manufacturing facility in the U.S.

“The 100% pharmaceutical tariff applies only to branded drugs, and constructing a factory may lead to an exemption. Many pharmaceutical companies have facilities in the U.S., so it may be relatively easy to superficially expand those facilities to avoid tariffs being applied,” UBS' Paul Donovan said.

data.title: European indexes rebound as investors shrug off fresh tariff fears
data.description:
London (UKX) +0.27% to 9,238. 

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.48% to 23,535. 

France (CAC:IND) +0.81% to 7,855.

Spain's GDP expands 3.1% Y/Y in Q2.

Sweden’s trade deficit widened to SEK 8.9 billion in August.

Retail sales in Denmark rose by 2.5% Y/Y in August.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) turned 0.71% lower to 549.93, snapping two sessions of losses. Investors largely shrugged off new U.S. tariff announcements from Trump, including a 100% levy on drugs, 25% on heavy trucks and more. Sector-wise, healthcare, technology, and telecoms lagged. 

The European Commission is reportedly planning to impose steep tariffs of 25%–50% on Chinese steel imports and related products in the coming weeks.

In the bond market, the yield on the US 10-year Treasury up less than 1 basis point to 4.18%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point to 2.77%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 4.75%. 

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)
